data.title: Canada to remove 100% tariffs on Chinese EV imports: prime minister
data.description:
Canada will remove the additional 100 per cent in tariffs it had imposed on Chinese electric vehicle (EV) imports, Prime Minister Mark Carney said during a state visit to Beijing.
In place of the higher duties, Carney said, Canada will cap import volume and apply a standard tariff rate of 6.1 per cent to shipments within the 49,000-unit limit.
More to follow...

data.title: Why Dali in China’s Yunnan province is such a great place for food adventurers
data.description:
Restaurant-goers in Dali, in China’s western Yunnan province, are spoiled by the historical city’s abundance of quality ingredients. Fridges stacked with seasonal vegetables, tofu concoctions and fresh cuts of meat are common sights for diners to browse, while fried innards and fish vie for attention in giant metal bowls. Aquatic vegetables soaking in water line floors. This is farm-to-table, Dali style.
Thanks to its temperate climate and diverse terrain, Yunnan is suitable for growing all manner of produce, from rice and avocados to tomatillos and blueberries. Its culinary practices are equally varied, shaped by the traditions of over 20 ethnic communities that converge there.
A two-hour train ride from the provincial capital Kunming, Dali, with its bountiful harvests and slower pace of life, is fertile ground for culinary experimentation.
Chang Hua, the chef behind Youshi, a modern Chinese restaurant, first visited the city after finishing culinary school in 2015, eager for a fresh start away from his home in Anhui province. He has called Dali home for over a decade.
His time in Yunnan has been a chain of experiments. He began with a makeshift catering business, delivering fried rice on his scooter. He later ran a couple of mala tang (spicy hotpot) businesses, private hotel kitchens and a small restaurant.

Inspired by a trip to Shanghai, where he tasted polished approaches to China’s southwestern cuisines, he opened Youshi in 2020.
“It was in Dali [that] I slowly found joy in cooking again,” Chang says.
The restaurant is located in a rustic house perched on a hill overlooking the expansive waters of Erhai Lake. Just behind the house lies its farm, which is dotted with an eclectic mix of produce: tall stalks of kale, low-lying pumpkins, plump eggplants and petite tomatillos all share the space.
Like many urban youths, Chang knew little about farming, but says the team is learning as they go. This newfound interest in cultivating the land is shared by many young chefs in Dali.
Tucked away in another corner of Dali is Trout Restaurant, named after the freshwater rainbow trout farmed in Yunnan. It was opened by chef-owner Xu Kai, a graduate of the Culinary Institute of America who went on to intern at Eleven Madison Park in New York City and Gaggan in Bangkok.
Xu first visited Dali in the summer of 2022. “The moment I tasted a local tomato, I knew this was it,” he says.

After a short stint in a Shenzhen kitchen, he returned in March 2023 to find a location for a restaurant, opening Trout Restaurant later that year.
Since then, Xu and his team have maintained the restaurant’s 400 square metre (4,300 sq ft) farm, where plants grow amid rambunctious chickens and a goat.
For Xu, the restaurant’s interior speaks of Yunnan as much as the food. The team spent four months transforming an old wood workshop into their new home. Tables and chairs were crafted from local timber, while lamps were fashioned from fish cages and bee buckets. To ensure an intimate experience, Trout has just four tables.
The herbs and plants grown on-site seep into the menu. A mini distiller transforms geranium leaves into essential oil, lemongrass is steeped with corn cobs for a soothing tea, and Vietnamese coriander is blended into a sauce to pair with local beef.
The farm is still under development, but the restaurant already uses its own eggs, and fresh goat’s milk is coming soon. As winter deepens, pumpkins, peppers and pea shoots begin to grace the tables, along with perennial herbs like perilla and lemon balm.
For both Chang and Xu, maintaining a farm is a way to understand how things grow, from germination to fruition. Neither claims expertise, but both clearly relish getting their hands dirty.

Beyond the farms stretches a vast universe of culinary possibilities, shaped by cultivated fields, foraged wilds, traditional diets and flavours imported from across the world.
“Yunnan has simply the best ingredients – the seasonality, the quality, the diversity,” Xu says. “We as chefs cook the ingredients, but it’s really the ingredients that teach and shape us.”
The tasting menu at Trout I had that evening featured a wide range of ingredients from the province, including sour honey (a traditional remedy of China’s Dai people), cured ham from Jianchuan county, redtail catfish caught in the Lancang River, and marbled pork from a local breed called dong gua zhu (winter melon pig).
“Many fine restaurants in bigger cities place a huge focus on ingredients from Yunnan,” Chang says. “We live and breathe here. We’re well positioned to showcase the quality of the produce here.”
Having lived in Dali for over a decade, Chang still sets out each November with his team to explore different corners of the province. Almost inevitably, a new flavour or technique will catch their attention.
Chang embraces the constraints and liberties of approaching Yunnan food as an outsider. “I am not making traditional Yunnan food. I try to work with local ingredients and borrow from local ways of cooking, but present them in new ways. Creating is what I’m good at.”

Xu views his restaurant as an introduction for those beginning to discover the richness of Yunnan’s food culture. He hopes to raise the profile of Yunnan in both China and the wider world as a gesture of gratitude to the land and people that took him in.
“Yunnan is such a welcoming place for young people from all over the country. When I first moved here, I stayed with a local Bai family, three generations under one roof. They were endlessly hospitable, always inviting me to join them for breakfast,” he says.
Xu and Chang are symbolic of a shift in Dali’s old town, where finding new restaurants run by young chefs from elsewhere was once unthinkable. Today, the city hums with fresh culinary ideas.
“It’s great to see more dynamism in the city, with all the new restaurants,” says Kang Hui, a Bai local who teaches cooking to visitors at the Linden Centre in Xizhou, a town about half an hour north of Dali by car.
“At the same time, to truly experience Dali, you have to go out to the local shops and eat to your heart’s content.” (Certain new establishments offer only fashionably tiny portions.)

According to Alice Zhang, head of leisure travel customer service at WildChina, years of lockdown due to the Covid pandemic pushed the industry to innovate.
“Yunnan food gradually came to the forefront,” Zhang says. “Everyone now knows about Yunnan coffee, which also took off during the pandemic. We’re now starting to introduce travellers to Yunnan wine as well.”
A thousand miles away in Hong Kong, Yunnan’s bounty is already widely enjoyed, from Pu’er tea to bowls of noodles at TamJai Yunnan Mixian. In November, its cuisine found an unlikely home at the newly opened Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui Hong Kong, with the launch of Jija by Vicky Lau, a bistro inspired by flavours from China’s southwestern provinces, including Yunnan and Guizhou.
Lau was captivated by Yunnan’s food on her first visit in 2023.
“How did I not know about Yunnan before? It’s every chef’s dream to find beautiful ingredients to work with year-round,” Lau says, cradling a box of hong guo shen, a sturdy berry grown in Yunnan.
Although a veteran of French cuisine, Lau is eager to cook from a different pantry. “Yunnan food has more freshness and acidity than other regional Chinese cuisines. The herbs, spices and dips are so different from what I’m used to in French cooking, with its sauces and meat jus,” she says.

Like Youshi in Dali, Jija openly adapts Yunnan flavours for local tastes. Yunnanese ingredients meet Hong Kong sensibilities: mushrooms appear in salads, soups and stir-fries, fresh herbs and spices season grilled meats, and a hearty fried rice with Yunnan cured ham, ganba mushrooms and Guizhou-style fried pork fat anchors the menu.
In Dali, Hong Kong and elsewhere across China, chefs who find themselves humbled by what Yunnan province offers are learning from locals and blending new flavours and techniques to create fresh culinary experiences.

data.title: China dominates global humanoid robot market with over 80% of installations
data.description:
China accounted for more than four out of five humanoid robot installations globally in 2025, driven by domestic start-ups AgiBot and Unitree Robotics, as mass production and commercialisation accelerated, according to a report by Counterpoint Research.
A total of 16,000 humanoid robots were installed globally in 2025, mainly for data collection and research, as well as in the logistics, manufacturing, and automotive sectors, according to Counterpoint data released on Wednesday.
The market was projected to exceed 100,000 units by 2027, a sixfold growth from 2025, with logistics, manufacturing and automotive uses expected to make up 72 per cent of all installations, the research firm said.
“The next two years will see more humanoid enterprises commercialising the mass-production versions of robots, and their performance will largely determine the development pace of the whole industry,” Counterpoint said.

Shanghai-based AgiBot led the market with a 30.4 per cent share of installations worldwide in 2025, followed by Hangzhou-based Unitree’s 26.4 per cent.
AgiBot’s diverse product range and open-source strategy helped drive commercial deployment across various sectors, while Unitree gained attention with its expertise in dynamic motion and balance control, Counterpoint said.
Shenzhen manufacturers UBTech Robotics and Leju Robotics ranked third and fourth with 5.2 per cent and 4.9 per cent, respectively. Elon Musk’s Tesla was fifth with 4.7 per cent as the production of Optimus Gen 2 and Gen 2.5 ramped up.
The Counterpoint report echoed earlier findings by Omdia, which ranked AgiBot and Unitree as the top firms by shipment volumes.
China’s humanoid robot sector has boomed in recent years with strong government support, though the National Development and Reform Commission, the country’s economic planning agency, warned of the risks of excessive duplication in November.
Firms have stepped up commercialisation efforts amid growing competition and formed partnerships with the broader manufacturing industry to enable large-scale deployment of robots on factory floors.
Counterpoint noted that some robotic firms were tapping into the household market with more affordable products that prioritise interaction over performing complex tasks.
“There is a vast potential across household scenarios, and enterprises would not like to leave it untapped,” the report said.
Robot rentals – led by AgiBot and Unitree – had also gained popularity as they lowered the cost of ownership and encouraged adoption in scenarios such as live performance, according to Counterpoint.

data.title: South Korean church’s ‘special force’ targets young Australians amid crackdown at home
data.description:
A controversial church facing possible disbandment over religious-political collusion in South Korea is aggressively expanding its presence across Australia by targeting young people at universities and shopping centres, according to a former member who has accused the group of weaponising fear to shield it from criticism.
The Shincheonji Church of Jesus, founded in 1984 by Lee Man-hee, who claims to be the second coming of Jesus Christ, has an estimated 200,000 followers in the East Asian nation.
According to Shincheonji’s website, the fringe Christian organisation has branches in more than 100 countries – including Australia, where it is deploying “special force teams” to boost recruitment.
While the church was already established in Melbourne, it has compiled extensive profiles of potential members in regional towns of Victoria and the Northern Territory, public broadcaster ABC reported.
Citing documents and a member who walked away from Shincheonji four months ago after being in the church for nearly three years, ABC said hobbies, life goals and personality traits were some of the features the church considered when drawing up a tailor-made plan for picking prospective disciples.


Cassie (not her real name) said members were subjected to a rigorous enrolment campaign that left them exhausted and sleep-deprived.
“[We would be] recruiting from early morning until late at night,” she said. “From the moment I would get on a train, I would fall asleep straight away … because I was so tired all the time.”
Cassie was introduced to Shincheonji by two members who asked her to join Bible study sessions.
She did not know about the church’s reputation as online searches were discouraged and deemed “spiritual suicide” in an attempt to prevent members from leaving.
“They find different ways to justify why looking or searching on the internet about criticism of Shincheonji is bad for your spirit,” Cassie said, adding that she quit the group after understanding the “flaws in the Shincheonji doctrine”.
In 2019, South Korean pastor Jo Mit-eum, who founded a firm to help educate people about such cult organisations, told This Week in Asia that Shincheonji “preaches to divorce from one’s spouse and other groups strongly advise people to quit their studies and jobs”.
Cassie said some members have dropped out of university to concentrate on special force teams working to widen Shincheonji’s base in Australia. This has prompted warnings from schools like Federation University and RMIT about the church’s “disingenuous and harmful” recruitment methods.

“A lot of these people have given up a lot for this church, so it is tough for them to let it go because their whole life is centred around the work,” she said.
Cassie’s revelation comes as a Victorian parliamentary panel is investigating coercive high-control groups such as Shincheonji based on nearly 300 submissions from ex-members and the families of current followers describing financial exploitation, job losses and fatigue.
According to a submission, the parents of a woman who joined Shincheonji Melbourne in 2021 said their daughter was an “entirely different person now”.
“She was once thriving, joyful and independent,” they said. “Now, she is financially dependent, socially isolated and deeply anxious. We fear for her life.”
Shincheonji intensified its overseas recruitment drive after an outbreak of coronavirus at a branch in South Korea’s Daegu triggered an exodus of members during the early days of the pandemic in 2020.
Last week, Seoul formed a task force to investigate Shincheonji and the Unification Church over alleged collusion between the groups and politicians, The Korea Times newspaper reported.
The outcome of the inquiry could result in their dissolution, with President Lee Jae Myung saying the “harm inflicted by such organisations on our society has been left unattended for far too long and has become severe”.
South Korea, where some 2 million Christians are involved with fringe churches, has in the past cracked down on cult groups.
In 2001, a religious establishment linked to a couple lost its corporate registration after it defrauded about 1,500 people.

data.title: China’s 7,000-year-old sunmao woodwork leads to world’s most efficient DNA editing tool
data.description:
How to beat the United States in gene editing? A research team from Peking University and the Chinese Academy of Sciences had struggled with this question for three years, with no results.
Then they decided to draw inspiration from an ancient woodwork tradition and cut DNA like wood. It led to a gene-editing tool with unprecedented efficiency.
In the Chinese mortise-and-tenon joint technique known as sunmao, no nails or glue are used. Instead, a protruding “tenon” on one piece fits securely into a matching “mortise” on another, creating a solid interlocking structure.
One of the earliest examples can be traced to China’s Hemudu culture in Zhejiang province around 7,000 years ago.
The new editing strategy achieves precise insertion and replacement efficiencies of up to 59.47 per cent in rice, surpassing those of existing mainstream tools.
At certain genomic loci, which refer to the fixed physical locations of genes or other DNA sequences on a chromosome, its editing efficiency is 10 times higher than that of traditional Cas9-based systems.
CRISPR-Cas9, the most popular tool in use, was pioneered by researchers affiliated with Harvard and MIT. Its core patents remain solid until 2038.
“Gene editing tools are like lithography machines; if they are ‘choked’, nothing gets done,’” Zhang Huawei, a researcher at the Institute of Advanced Agricultural Sciences of Peking University, said in a January 6, interview with China Science Daily.
“Just as we need our own lithography machines, we need independently developed tools for gene editing.”
In December 2019, he established his own laboratory at the university with a goal to create a framework for precise gene editing with independent intellectual property rights for China.


In crop breeding, precise and scarless DNA fragment insertion or replacement represents a pivotal technology for overcoming bottlenecks in varietal improvement and improving national food security.
However, for the first three years, the experiments yielded no usable data. Then the ancient mortise-and-tenon structure sparked a new idea.
The techniques of Chinese wooden architecture, including mortise-and-tenon joints, have been inscribed on Unesco’s Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity.
Zhang realised that the mortise-and-tenon principle closely resembled the gene-editing framework they sought: achieving precise integration by constructing complementary “tenons” and “mortises” at the DNA level.
The team engineered a special double-strand break structure – forming a “mortise” – at target sites in the rice genome, featuring a defined overhang. They then synthesised a double-stranded DNA donor that served as the “tenon”, with ends complementary to the “mortise”.
Through precise pairing and ligation, their “mortise-tenon connection system” (MT system) enables directional insertion and replacement of DNA fragments.
At a single target locus, the MT system’s forward insertion efficiency reached 46.55 per cent and 48.12 per cent, with no reverse insertion. In contrast, the traditional Cas9 system paired with standard donors achieved only 1 per cent efficiency.
At optimal sites, the MT system’s forward insertion efficiency hit 83.54 per cent, with a precise insertion rate of 59.47 per cent – more than 10 times that of the Cas9 system.
Beyond higher efficiency, the MT system also enables “scarless editing”. Previous systems often relied on recombinases, which leave short recognition sequences at insertion sites – akin to surgical scars.
“Our mortise-tenon system doesn’t require such sequences; after editing, it leaves no extra bases behind,” Zhang added.

data.title: Malaysia rules 15-year-old fit to stand trial in 2025 school murder of Yap Shing Xuen
data.description:
The 15-year-old male student accused of murdering his schoolmate at a school in Malaysia’s Selangor state has been declared fit to stand trial, following a psychiatric evaluation.
His lawyer, Kitson Foong, informed the press that he conveyed the report’s findings to the Magistrate’s Court during closed-door proceedings on January 16. The court subsequently ruled that the accused is fit to stand trial and enter his defense.
According to the lawyer, the psychiatric report also recommended the teen be held at a correctional centre in Puncak Alam, described as a “prison for children”.
According to Foong, the court ordered the accused to attend counselling and therapy on a monthly basis.
Magistrate Amira Sariaty Zainal scheduled the case for mention on February 6.
The teenager – whose identity is withheld due to his age – is charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code for a crime committed between 9.20am and 9.35am on October 14, 2025.
He is accused of the brutal killing of Yap Shing Xuen, who was found dead in the toilet of a school in Bandar Utama. Her mother, Wong Lee Ping, stated that the victim had been stabbed more than 200 times from her neck to her thigh.

During an investigation, police recovered two knives and a kerambit – a small, curved blade often associated with martial arts – from the suspect’s bag.
If convicted, the charge carries either the death penalty or a prison sentence of 30 to 40 years, along with at least 12 strokes of the cane.
However, as the accused is under 18 years old, any sentencing would be at the discretion of Malaysia’s king.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Hong Kong’s finance chief warns of market volatility, pledges support for families
data.description:
Hong Kong’s capital market is likely to experience significant fluctuations this year owing to intensifying geopolitical risks, the city’s finance chief has warned, stressing the need for caution in financial management.
Six weeks before the government unveils its annual budget, Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po pledged to consider whether there is scope to adjust child allowance to encourage more births, after a Post report revealed that Hong Kong’s registered births hit a record low last year.
During a briefing for lawmakers on Friday, Chan reported that the economic growth for last year is forecast at 3.2 per cent despite geopolitical pressures. While export performance remained strong, consumer spending had weakened, he said.
For the coming year, Chan expressed “cautious optimism” about the economic outlook, citing risks that could affect financial security but also highlighted the improving economy in mainland China.
“Caution is needed because we anticipate that geopolitical risks will only intensify. Under such circumstances, the capital market is inevitably subject to significant fluctuations,” he said at a special meeting of the Legislative Council’s finance committee. “Geopolitical factors influence capital flows. We must exercise caution.
“While we must vigorously pursue development, we also need to coordinate efforts on security, particularly in financial safety, to prevent unexpected disruptions and ensure financial stability.”
He acknowledged that the recent improvement in the city’s economy, together with the mainland’s stable growth, brought some optimism.
Chan also said he expected the United States to reduce its interest rates once or twice this year, leading to a looser financial environment conducive to business and market development.

However, he added that income from land premiums over the next two to three years would not be sufficient to cover investment in public works – estimated at HK$120 billion (US$15.4 billion) – and that the shortfall would have to be met through bond issuances.
“We have to make use of market funds to invest in our future and infrastructure,” he said.
The Post previously reported that the number of registered births in the city last year hit a record low of 31,714.
Lawmaker Nixie Lam Lam said the low birth rate could not be reversed by the baby bonus – a one-time cash reward of HK$20,000 offered by the government for each eligible baby born from October 2023 – as couples would not decide to have children simply for the one-off payment.
She said more child care support services should be provided for parents so that children would not be regarded as a financial burden.
Lawmaker Chris Ip Ngo-tung asked whether the government could increase the child allowance to relieve the burden on the middle class.
Chan replied: “This year, regarding tax and the child allowance, we will later conduct a comprehensive review of adjustments to different allowances over the past eight to 10 years to see if there is any room [to increase the child allowance].”
The government last raised the child allowance in 2023, from HK$120,000 to HK$130,000.
Chan acknowledged that raising children was not easy, but a lifelong commitment.
“We very much hope we can support different families, particularly young families,” he said.
Chan is scheduled to deliver the budget speech on February 25.

data.title: China’s ‘trillion-yuan districts’ reveal gains and gaps of its hi-tech push
data.description:
The anticipated rise of Shenzhen’s Nanshan district to a “trillion-yuan district” marks a monumental milestone, placing it alongside Shanghai’s Pudong and Beijing’s Haidian as the only districts to reach such a GDP threshold.
Beyond a mere numerical benchmark, the “trillion-yuan” label signifies China’s transition into a technology-powered economic era. This achievement underscores more than local success – it amplifies the resilience of China’s economy, increasingly driven by hi-tech innovation. The pace and quality of the nation’s future growth will depend on the ability of these knowledge-intensive hubs to innovate, navigate global headwinds and seize opportunities in emerging industries.
Nanshan district exemplifies China’s shift from “Made in China” to “Created in China”. Looking at the broader “trillion-yuan club”, a common thread emerges: each district is powered by the knowledge economy and technological innovation.
Across China’s hi-tech parks, energy is palpable. Passionate entrepreneurs turn fresh ideas into viable ventures; debates fill cafes, focused on the next breakthrough. This dynamism reflects economic vitality, as evidenced by a steady surge in new tech firms.
Official statistics show more than 1 million new enterprises were established in China’s emerging and future industries from January to November 2025. This included 775,000 firms in sectors such as information technology and advanced manufacturing. Future industries – a category spanning healthcare, artificial intelligence (AI) and energy – saw growth of 35.8 per cent, adding 283,000 new companies.
China’s strategic shift towards hi-tech innovation as a primary economic driver showed early success in 2025. Sectors such as new energy vehicles, industrial robots, AI and advanced electronics are contributing substantially to GDP.


The hi-tech sector’s share of China’s GDP rose to an estimated 16.8 per cent last year and is projected to reach 18.3 per cent this year, nearly double its 2017 level. This transformation highlights how hi-tech industries are becoming the economy’s main shock absorber, offsetting slowdowns in traditional pillars such as real estate and low-end manufacturing.
The Chinese economy looks more resilient than it feels. At the same time, it feels more stagnant than it looks. There are two sides to the story of China’s economy.
In 2025, China’s property sector remained mired in a severe downturn. The prolonged slump has eroded household wealth, strained local government finances and heightened risks for the banking sector. Notably, banks have been reluctant at times to push heavily indebted developers into bankruptcy, opting to keep their financial distress undisclosed rather than triggering formal insolvency.
China’s economy can be accurately described as one of “ice and fire” – a stark contrast depending on the sector or individual in question. This divergence has intensified since the end of the Covid-19 pandemic lockdowns. While headline GDP growth has continued to meet its annual target of around 5 per cent, the benefits of this expansion have failed to translate into broad-based public confidence or prosperity.




The “ice” is palpable. A deepening property downturn and mounting local government debt continue to suppress economic vitality, eroding household wealth, weakening employment prospects and dampening consumer sentiment. Consequently, growth in household consumption has slowed markedly.
Meanwhile, the “fire” burns in hi-tech and advanced manufacturing sectors, which are expanding rapidly in terms of output and investment. Yet these industries, though they add high amounts of value, have a limited capacity to absorb labour. Their gains are often concentrated, leaving ordinary citizens with little direct sense of participation or benefit. As a result, the dynamism in these sectors feels disconnected from the daily economic reality of many people, creating a persistent disconnect between measured growth and lived experience.
Even for China’s most successful electric vehicle makers, profit margins remain low and under pressure. Intense domestic price competition, significant overcapacity and the reduction of government subsidies have eroded profitability across the sector. In 2025, the overall operating margin for China’s auto industry was less than 5 per cent. BYD, one of China’s most profitable carmakers, reported a margin of 5.2 per cent in 2024, less than half of Toyota’s 11.9 per cent operating profit margin.
Beyond profitability concerns, major US and Western technology firms are moving to reduce their footprint in mainland China. This retreat is visible in key innovation hubs such as Shanghai’s Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park. Microsoft closed its IoT & AI Insider Lab there in early 2025 and Amazon closed its AI research lab in July of that year. The trend extends to other giants such as IBM, which significantly scaled back its China research division and laid off more than a thousand employees.

Geopolitical tensions are now pushing even China’s brightest tech stars to abandon their roots. A prominent example is the Chinese AI start-up Manus, which was acquired by Meta to bolster its advanced AI capabilities. Manus has been focused on developing a general-purpose AI agent designed for research, automation and complex tasks.
With Meta’s landmark US$2.5 billion acquisition, however, it is no longer a Chinese company. Even before the acquisition, Manus closed its Beijing office and moved to Singapore. The firm now has offices in San Francisco and Tokyo, with another in Paris expected to open soon.
The ascent of China’s “trillion-yuan districts” tells an exciting story of transformation – a decisive pivot towards a hi-tech-driven economy. The true measure of this progress, however, will be its translation into shared prosperity, ensuring that every citizen can feel its benefits in their daily lives.

data.title: City table tennis player Chan to compete in men’s doubles at Singapore Smash for first time
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Baldwin Chan Ho-wah will take a step into the unknown next month at the Singapore Smash, when he competes in table tennis men’s doubles for the first time at the prestigious event.
One half of the world’s top pairing, alongside compatriot Wong Chun-ting, Chan has only previously played in the singles competition at the city’s sports hub, The Kallang.
The duo had a strong 2025, winning the WTT Contender Zagreb and fighting back to beat China’s Lin Shidong and Huang Youzheng in the final of Europe Smash.
“I’ve never actually played doubles at Singapore Smash before,” Chan said. “I really hope I can do so this time and experience the atmosphere again. The last two times I played there, I could really feel the support from the Singaporean fans, and that left a strong impression on me.”
Beating the Chinese pair in August lifted the Hongkongers to the top of the rankings, just ahead of South Korea’s Lim Jong-hoon and An Jae-hyun .

Looking ahead, Chan believes the partnership with Wong is only getting stronger as they continue their pursuit of further success on the WTT stage.
“We got better and better in the second half of this year, and we hope to continue that momentum,” Chan said. “If we can keep improving, fans will be able to see better table tennis from us, and hopefully that will lead to more silverware at different competitions.”
While the 21-year-old’s focus will be on continuing his strong doubles form with Wong, Chan is also aiming to make progress in the highly competitive singles field, where his first goal is to make the main draw. After that, a medal and to improve his world ranking.
“Grand Smash events are always very tough, so I can only take it match by match,” Chan, who is ranked 71st in singles, said. “As long as I give my best, that’s all I can ask for.”
Hong Kong will be represented in the women’s tournament too, with Ng Wing-lam out to battle through the qualification rounds of the US$1.55 million event.
The women’s world No 92 said she would take it “match by match” rather than setting a target.
“Depending on the draw, I hope I can qualify for the main draw,” she said. “My ranking isn’t very high right now, but it has been steadily improving. I want to keep progressing without focusing too heavily on results and try to enjoy the process.”

data.title: China warns of ‘honey trap’ risks in defence sector after official jailed for espionage
data.description:
Chinese state security authorities recently warned of “honey trap” risks in the defence and technology sectors, citing a case in which a public official was blackmailed into surrendering state secrets following a trip abroad.
A social media account affiliated with the National Administration of State Secrets Protection published an article on Wednesday stating that personnel in the military industry were becoming targets of honey traps by foreign intelligence agencies and provided details of a related case.
According to the account called Baomiguan, a project manager surnamed Guo from a mainland military enterprise was on a work trip to a foreign city to accept a project. While abroad, a spy posing as a “business representative” approached Guo via deliberately arranged social occasions.
“This seemingly accidental encounter was actually a honey trap orchestrated by a foreign intelligence agency,” the article said.


After Guo had sexual relations with a woman working for the spy’s organisation, it threatened to expose Guo’s affair and successfully recruited him, it reported.
Subsequently, Guo accepted tasks assigned by the organisation to help in recruiting a Chinese national surnamed Chen, a technical expert from a company, and another Chinese national surnamed Li, a staff member.
The three provided the organisation with internal information and classified details about their military-industrial enterprise, according to the article.
“Throughout the intelligence exchange, Guo acted as the on-site interpreter,” it said.
China’s law on guarding state secrets has a three-tier system for classified information: confidential, secret and top secret.
In this case, 12 documents reportedly leaked by Guo were categorised as confidential, and two documents provided by Chen and Li were also confidential.
The article said Guo was ultimately sentenced to 10 years in prison for espionage, and Chen and Li were also held criminally liable.
It did not say where the case took place or when.

This is not the first time that security agencies have warned against honey traps during trips abroad, but details are rarely revealed in such cases involving the military industry.
The article cautioned against shortcomings in internal security management within some organisations, particularly in perfunctory visit abroad reviews and a lack of oversight abroad.
It also offered suggestions to improve supervision, management and preventive education for personnel travelling abroad.
“[We should] focus on enhancing the awareness and political discernment of personnel visiting abroad through typical cases and interactive experiential education,” the article stated.
It further proposed implementing full-cycle management for personnel travelling abroad, from pre-trip reviews and supervision abroad to post-return observation.
It also suggested strengthening policies that require state employees to declare their relationships abroad.
China’s Ministry of State Security has repeatedly told mainland officials, students and tourists to be wary of recruitment traps abroad set by foreign intelligence agencies amid Beijing’s fierce competition with Washington and its Western allies.
In May 2024, the ministry disclosed a case in which a Chinese provincial government official was targeted in a honey trap while travelling abroad.
